title: Staphylococcus epidermidis: coagulase-negative
vignette: A 52-year-old man is hospitalized due to 2 weeks of low-grade fever, malaise, anorexia, and fatigue. The patient has a history of bicuspid aortic valve and underwent aortic valve replacement a year ago. Physical examination reveals a new regurgitation murmur. Blood cultures repeatedly grow gram-positive cocci in clusters, which are identified as Staphylococcus epidermidis. This pathogen most likely demonstrates which of the following characteristics?

clues.text: aortic valve replacement a year ago
clues.note: A prosthetic valve is the key risk factor: Staphylococcus epidermidis adheres to and forms biofilm on artificial surfaces, causing prosthetic-valve endocarditis and device infections.
A new regurgitant murmur plus subacute fever signals infective endocarditis — here on the prosthetic valve. Repeated positive cultures confirm true infection rather than contamination.
Gram-positive cocci in clusters = Staphylococcus (vs streptococci in chains). Staph are catalase-positive; speciation then uses coagulase.
S. epidermidis is a coagulase-NEGATIVE, novobiocin-SENSITIVE skin commensal that infects prosthetic material via biofilm. This identity directly drives the lab-characteristic answer.

whatMatters: Once the organism is named S. epidermidis, the question is just its lab fingerprint. Staphylococci are catalase-positive; S. epidermidis is coagulase-NEGATIVE (unlike S. aureus) and novobiocin-sensitive — so 'negative coagulase test' is the defining feature.
strategy: For 'which characteristic' microbiology stems, run the identification algorithm: gram-positive cocci in clusters → catalase + (Staph) → coagulase splits S. aureus (+) from coagulase-negative staph (S. epidermidis, S. saprophyticus) → novobiocin splits epidermidis (sensitive) from saprophyticus (resistant).

eliminations.label: alpha hemolysis — that's viridans strep
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: alpha (partial/green) hemolysis on blood agar is characteristic of viridans streptococci and S. pneumoniae. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: S. epidermidis is gamma-hemolytic (non-hemolytic), and hemolysis isn't its identifier. ✅ Correct if: the organism were a viridans streptococcus (subacute endocarditis on a native valve after dental work).

eliminations.label: mannitol fermentation — that's S. aureus
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: mannitol fermentation (yellow on mannitol salt agar) is typical of S. aureus. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: S. epidermidis does NOT ferment mannitol; this trait points to aureus, not epidermidis. ✅ Correct if: the organism were S. aureus.

eliminations.label: negative catalase — that's streptococci
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a catalase-NEGATIVE gram-positive coccus is a streptococcus/enterococcus. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: ALL staphylococci (including S. epidermidis) are catalase-POSITIVE; calling it catalase-negative contradicts the genus. ✅ Correct if: the organism were a Streptococcus/Enterococcus.

eliminations.label: novobiocin resistance — that's S. saprophyticus
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: novobiocin RESISTANCE distinguishes S. saprophyticus (a cause of UTIs in young women). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: S. epidermidis is novobiocin-SENSITIVE; resistance points to saprophyticus instead. ✅ Correct if: the coagulase-negative staph caused a UTI and was novobiocin-resistant (saprophyticus).

eliminations.label: yellow pigment — that's S. aureus (aureus = gold)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a golden-yellow pigment is the namesake of S. aureus ('aureus' = gold). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: S. epidermidis colonies are white, not pigmented; pigment indicates aureus. ✅ Correct if: the organism were S. aureus.

reasoning: Subacute prosthetic-valve endocarditis with repeated positive cultures
Identified as S. epidermidis = coagulase-negative, novobiocin-sensitive
Defining lab feature = negative coagulase test → answer D
mechanism: Staphylococcus epidermidis is a low-virulence skin commensal that produces adhesins and a polysaccharide biofilm, letting it colonize prosthetic valves, catheters, and joints. Like all staph it is catalase-positive, but unlike S. aureus it is coagulase-negative (can't clot plasma) and is novobiocin-sensitive. Repeatedly positive cultures plus a prosthetic device shift it from 'contaminant' to true pathogen.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > gram-positive cocci algorithm: catalase (Staph + / Strep −), coagulase (S. aureus + / CoNS −), novobiocin (S. epidermidis sensitive / S. saprophyticus resistant). Note epidermidis = biofilm on prosthetics.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The organism is already named, so the question only wants its position in the identification tree. Recall S. epidermidis = catalase +, coagulase −, novobiocin-sensitive, white, gamma-hemolytic.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on catalase vs coagulase, hemolysis types, novobiocin testing, biofilm, commensal vs contaminant? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Every wrong option is a real staph/strep trait planted on the wrong bug (mannitol/pigment = aureus; catalase−/alpha = strep; novobiocin-R = saprophyticus). Match each test to the species before picking.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: If you mixed up coagulase vs catalase, build a one-line ladder: clusters → catalase → coagulase → novobiocin, and rehearse where each species falls.

keywords.front: Coagulase-negative, novobiocin-sensitive staph on a prosthetic valve
Coagulase test result for S. epidermidis

firstAid.detail: Micro > gram-positive cocci: S. epidermidis (coagulase −, novobiocin-sensitive, biofilm, prosthetic-device/valve infections, common blood-culture contaminant).
firstAid.location: Micro > gram-positive cocci: S. epidermidis (coagulase −, novobiocin-sensitive, biofilm, prosthetic-device/valve infections, common blood-culture contaminant).

firstAid.detail: Cardio/Micro > endocarditis: prosthetic-valve endocarditis (S. epidermidis early; S. aureus), subacute (viridans strep), culture criteria (Duke).
firstAid.location: Cardio/Micro > endocarditis: prosthetic-valve endocarditis (S. epidermidis early; S. aureus), subacute (viridans strep), culture criteria (Duke).

firstAid.detail: Micro > catalase, coagulase, novobiocin, hemolysis, mannitol fermentation — the gram-positive-coccus algorithm distinguishing aureus, epidermidis, saprophyticus, and streptococci.
firstAid.location: Micro > catalase, coagulase, novobiocin, hemolysis, mannitol fermentation — the gram-positive-coccus algorithm distinguishing aureus, epidermidis, saprophyticus, and streptococci.

memoryHook: epidermidis = skin bug that gloms onto plastic; coagulase-NEGative, novobiocin-NICE (sensitive). aureus is the gold, mannitol-loving, coagulase-positive one.
